symbols.name: n
symbols.def: Number of experimental units.

symbols.name: U_n
symbols.def: Index set of the fixed finite population.

symbols.name: Z
symbols.def: Random treatment-assignment vector.

symbols.name: p_i
symbols.def: Known Bernoulli probability P_Z(Z_i=1).

symbols.name: P_Z
symbols.def: Known law of Z induced by the experimental design.

symbols.name: N_i
symbols.def: Known assignment neighborhood used by unit i's exposure mapping.

symbols.name: E_n
symbols.def: Set of analyst-specified exposure labels.

symbols.name: a
symbols.def: First exposure label defining the causal contrast.

symbols.name: b
symbols.def: Second exposure label defining the causal contrast, with b not equal to a.

symbols.name: c_i
symbols.def: Known map from assignments in N_i to unit i's exposure label.

symbols.name: Y_i(z)
symbols.def: Fixed potential outcome of unit i under full assignment z.

symbols.name: Y_i(e)
symbols.def: Fixed potential outcome of unit i under exposure label e.

symbols.name: Y_i^{obs}
symbols.def: Observed outcome Y_i(Z).

symbols.name: I_i(e)
symbols.def: Indicator 1{c_i(Z_{N_i})=e}.

symbols.name: pi_i(e)
symbols.def: Known design probability P_Z[I_i(e)=1].

symbols.name: pi_exp
symbols.def: Common lower bound for the two target exposure probabilities.

symbols.name: theta
symbols.def: Average fixed-potential-outcome contrast n^{-1} sum_{i in U_n}[Y_i(a)-Y_i(b)].

symbols.name: theta_0
symbols.def: Candidate value of theta tested by an e-value.

symbols.name: X_i
symbols.def: I_i(a)Y_i^{obs}/pi_i(a)-I_i(b)Y_i^{obs}/pi_i(b).

symbols.name: theta_hat
symbols.def: n^{-1} sum_{i in U_n} X_i.

symbols.name: D_n
symbols.def: Graph with edge {i,j} exactly when i!=j and N_i intersect N_j is nonempty.

symbols.name: chi_f(D_n)
symbols.def: inf_{r>=1} chi_r(D_n)/r, where chi_r(D_n) is the least number of colors in an r-fold proper coloring of D_n.

symbols.name: kappa_n
symbols.def: kappa_n=chi_f(D_n).

symbols.name: Vbar_n
symbols.def: Vbar_n=n^{-1}sum_{i in U_n}[1/pi_i(a)+1/pi_i(b)].

symbols.name: lambda
symbols.def: Positive tuning parameter for a fixed-time exponential test.

symbols.name: s
symbols.def: Sign indexing an upper or lower tail exponential test.

symbols.name: mathcal E_lambda^s(theta_0)
symbols.def: Exponential test statistic indexed by a candidate contrast theta_0.

symbols.name: alpha
symbols.def: Target randomization miscoverage probability.

symbols.name: C_alpha
symbols.def: Two-sided interval centered at theta_hat with the Bernstein half-width defined in def:confidence-interval.

symbols.name: w_alpha
symbols.def: Half-width sqrt{2 kappa_n Vbar_n log(2/alpha)/n}+kappa_n log(2/alpha)/(3n pi_exp).

symbols.name: r
symbols.def: Common number of units in each block of the canonical clique-block benchmark.

symbols.name: B
symbols.def: Number of blocks B=n/r in the canonical clique-block benchmark.

symbols.name: mathcal B_n
symbols.def: Known partition of U_n into B labeled blocks of size r in the canonical clique-block benchmark.

symbols.name: B_l
symbols.def: The l-th block in the known benchmark partition mathcal B_n.

symbols.name: h
symbols.def: Exposure label assigned when a block is neither all treated nor all control.

symbols.name: pi_star
symbols.def: Known common target-exposure probability pi_star=2^{-r} in the balanced canonical clique-block benchmark.

symbols.name: c_0
symbols.def: Universal constant c_0=1/4096 in the proved moderate-deviation condition.

symbols.name: c_1
symbols.def: Universal constant c_1=1/(256 sqrt(2)) in the proved confidence-length lower bound.

symbols.name: alpha_0
symbols.def: Universal cutoff alpha_0=exp(-1024) in the proved confidence-length comparison.

symbols.name: mathcal M_n
symbols.def: Class of n-unit Bernoulli exposure experiments satisfying the listed member properties.

symbols.name: mathcal M_{n,r}^{block}
symbols.def: Nonhomogeneous subclass of mathcal M_n satisfying the equal block partition, balanced Bernoulli assignment, unanimous target-exposure map, and exact positivity calibration; block-homogeneous target schedules form only a lower-bound witness subclass.

symbols.name: mathfrak C_alpha
symbols.space: maps from observed data to intervals
symbols.def: Procedures C with inf_{m in mathcal M_{n,r}^{block}} P_{Z,m}{theta(m) in C(Z,Y^{obs})} >= 1-alpha for the canonical nonhomogeneous block class.

assumptions.condition: For every i in U_n and z in {0,1}^n, Y_i(z)=Y_i(c_i(z_{N_i})).
The array (Y_i(e): i in U_n, e in E_n) is nonrandom under P_Z.
For every i in U_n and e in E_n, 0<=Y_i(e)<=1.
r divides n and mathcal B_n=(B_1,...,B_B) is a partition of U_n with |B_l|=r for every l in {1,...,B}.
p_i=1/2 for every i in U_n.
For every l in {1,...,B} and i in B_l, N_i=B_l and c_i(z_{B_l}) equals a if z_j=1 for every j in B_l, equals b if z_j=0 for every j in B_l, and equals h otherwise.
For every l in {1,...,B}, i,j in B_l, and e in {a,b}, Y_i(e)=Y_j(e).

definitions.name: General bounded Bernoulli exposure-experiment class
definitions.construction: mathcal M_n={m=(P_Z,(N_i,c_i,(Y_i(e))_{e in E_n})_{i in U_n}) : m satisfies ass:bernoulli-design, ass:exposure-sufficiency, ass:fixed-schedule, ass:bounded-outcomes, and ass:target-positivity for this n}

definitions.name: Exposure-dependency graph
definitions.construction: D_n=(U_n,{{i,j}:i!=j and N_i intersect N_j is nonempty})

definitions.name: Exact fractional-color representation
definitions.construction: kappa_n=chi_f(D_n)=min_{r>=1}chi_r(D_n)/r, where chi_r(D_n)=min{q: there exist independent sets S_1,...,S_q of D_n with each i in U_n belonging to exactly r of them}

definitions.name: Fractional-coloring Bernstein e-value
definitions.construction: mathcal E_lambda^s(theta_0)=exp{s lambda n(theta_hat-theta_0)-lambda^2 kappa_n n Vbar_n/[2(1-lambda kappa_n/(3 pi_exp))]}

definitions.name: Fractional-coloring Bernstein interval
definitions.construction: C_alpha=[theta_hat-w_alpha,theta_hat+w_alpha], where w_alpha=sqrt{2 kappa_n Vbar_n log(2/alpha)/n}+kappa_n log(2/alpha)/(3n pi_exp)

definitions.name: Canonical nonhomogeneous clique-block benchmark class
definitions.construction: mathcal M_{n,r}^{block}={m in mathcal M_n : m satisfies ass:block-partition, ass:balanced-block-assignment, ass:hard-block-exposure, and ass:block-positivity-calibration for this n and r}

definitions.name: Uniformly valid intervals over the canonical clique-block class
definitions.construction: mathfrak C_alpha={C:(Z,(Y_i^{obs})_{i in U_n})->intervals in R : inf_{m in mathcal M_{n,r}^{block}}P_{Z,m}[theta(m) in C(Z,Y^{obs})]>=1-alpha}

statements.statement: For every \(m\in\mathcal M_n\) and \(i\in U_n\), define \(W_i=X_i-\{Y_i(a)-Y_i(b)\}\). Then \(\mathbb E_{P_Z}[\widehat{\theta}]=\theta\), \(\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i]=0\), and \[\sum_{i\in U_n}W_i=n(\widehat{\theta}-\theta).\] The graph \(D_n\) is a dependency graph for both \((W_i)_{i\in U_n}\) and \((X_i)_{i\in U_n}\). Moreover, for every \(i\in U_n\), \[|W_i|\le\pi_{\exp}^{-1},\qquad \mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\le\pi_i(a)^{-1}+\pi_i(b)^{-1},\] and \[\sum_{i\in U_n}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\le n\overline V_n.\]
statements.justification: This lemma establishes exact design-unbiasedness and exposes the centered summands' dependency graph, range, and second-moment envelope. It is foundational exposure-HT algebra in the Aronow--Samii framework and carries no novelty claim.
statements.gap: The lemma closes bookkeeping needed to specialize established fractional-color concentration to the causal exposure contrast; it is not a confidence-length lower bound, rare-reveal reduction, or same-class minimax comparison.
statements.consumer: The Holder--Bernstein step consumes these explicit local bounds. In the paper-wide comparator map, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson supply concentration substrate, while the new contribution lies downstream in exact homogeneous-witness transport and the attained same-class interval-length sandwich. The interval remains valid over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, while exact compression and lower-bound witnessing occur only on the homogeneous subclass.

statements.statement: For every m in mathcal M_n, every s in {-1,1}, and every 0<lambda<3 pi_exp/kappa_n, E_{P_Z}[mathcal E_lambda^s(theta)]<=1.
statements.justification: This lemma specializes exact-fold Holder aggregation to the exposure-HT summands. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang supplies that aggregation mechanism, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) supplies closely related generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration, so no novelty is claimed for the exponential mechanism itself.
statements.gap: The lemma is not an exposure-class confidence-length lower bound. Aronow--Samii supplies the causal HT setting; Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies aligned effective-count intuition under SUTVA; the paper's added step is the later exact rare-reveal witness and same-class sandwich.
statements.consumer: It supplies the finite-sample upper benchmark. That role is distinct from Leung's asymptotic approximate-interference inference, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's variance optimization, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary intervals. The resulting upper validity extends over nonhomogeneous schedules; only the later exact reduction and lower-bound witness impose block homogeneity.

statements.statement: For every m in mathcal M_n, P_{Z,m}{theta in C_alpha}>=1-alpha; equivalently, P_{Z,m}{|theta_hat-theta|>sqrt{2 kappa_n Vbar_n log(2/alpha)/n}+kappa_n log(2/alpha)/(3n pi_exp)}<=alpha.
statements.justification: Chernoff inversion gives a computable randomization-valid interval over the full nonhomogeneous exposure class. Its upper validity is useful, but the fractional-color Bernstein form is established substrate in light of Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3), not the paper's priority claim.
statements.gap: Aronow--Samii provides exposure-HT inference but not the later confidence-length lower bound. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith proves SUTVA minimax RMSE rates, not uniformly valid expected interval length with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), network exposures, or exact rare-reveal transport. Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens likewise address different asymptotic, variance, sequential, design, concentration, or exact-interval questions.
statements.consumer: After restriction to the clique-block class and intersection with the causal parameter range, this proposition supplies the attainable upper endpoint. The matching lower endpoint uses only homogeneous witnesses inside the larger nonhomogeneous class, so the eventual novelty is the exact witness transport and same-class sandwich, not a general-network minimax claim or separate necessity of both penalties.

statements.statement: If D_n has no edges, then kappa_n=1 and the interval in prop:known-holder-bernstein-ci is the ordinary independent-summand Bernstein interval for the exposure-HT contrast with variance envelope Vbar_n and range bound pi_exp^{-1}.
statements.justification: This boundary check shows exact reduction to ordinary independent-summand Bernstein inference when \(\chi_f(D_n)=1\). It has no priority claim.
statements.gap: The proposition verifies constants and dependence normalization only; it does not extend Aronow--Samii, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, Janson, Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith, Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, or Rigdon--Hudgens on their respective causal, concentration, RMSE, asymptotic, variance, sequential, design, partial-interference, or exact-interval results.
statements.consumer: It supports interpretation of the upper benchmark only. The paper's substantive addition remains the homogeneous-witness rare-reveal transport that certifies a lower bound against procedures valid on the larger nonhomogeneous class and thereby yields the attained same-class expected-length sandwich.

statements.statement: The subclass relation \(\mathcal M_{n,r}^{\mathrm{block}}\subseteq\mathcal M_n\) holds, and \(\theta(m)\in[-1,1]\) for every \(m\in\mathcal M_{n,r}^{\mathrm{block}}\). Moreover, for every \(m\in\mathcal M_{n,r}^{\mathrm{block}}\), \(D_n\) is the disjoint union of \(B=n/r\) cliques of size \(r\), \(\kappa_n=r\), \(\pi_i(a)=\pi_i(b)=\pi_\star=2^{-r}\) for every \(i\in U_n\), \(\pi_{\exp}=2^{-r}\), and \(\overline V_n=2^{r+1}\).
statements.justification: The strengthened geometry lemma records the ambient subclass relation, bounded target, clique-block dependency geometry, exposure probabilities, and propensity envelope for every member of the nonhomogeneous class.
statements.gap: Its conclusions require no block-homogeneous outcome restriction; homogeneity belongs only to the lower-bound witness.
statements.consumer: It is the public interface supplying every class, clipping, graph, and propensity substitution used by the comparison theorem's upper construction.

statements.statement: Do universal constants c_0,c_1>0 and alpha_0 in (0,1/4] exist such that, for every alpha in (0,alpha_0), every n and r with r dividing n and r 2^r log(1/alpha)<=c_0 n, and every C in mathfrak C_alpha, sup_{m in mathcal M_{n,r}^{block}}E_{Z,m}[length(C)]>=c_1 sqrt{r 2^r log(1/alpha)/n}? This is a lower bound for the square-root term only; the displayed moderate-deviation regime makes the linear term r 2^r log(1/alpha)/n no larger than a constant multiple of that term.
statements.justification: The cited upper benchmark is informative only if the r-fold graph penalty and rare-exposure factor cannot be eliminated by another uniformly randomization-valid procedure.
statements.gap: SandovalEtAl2026 gives lower-bound logic for ordinary randomized experiments. TchetgenTchetgenVanderWeele2012 gives Hoeffding-type finite-sample intervals under partial or stratified interference, and RigdonHudgens2015 gives exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference; neither supplies a confidence-length lower bound for repeated all-treated/all-control neighborhood exposures.
statements.consumer: A positive answer certifies the square-root effective-exposure loss in a concrete general-network submodel and identifies when improved intervals must exploit assumptions beyond bounded finite-population schedules.

sampling_model.design: Known independent Bernoulli assignment P_Z=product_i Bernoulli(p_i).
sampling_model.units: A fixed finite population U_n with fixed bounded exposure-indexed potential outcomes.
sampling_model.treatment: Binary assignment Z_i and known local exposure c_i(Z_{N_i}).
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: None; the interval uses only known design probabilities and observed outcomes.

estimand_functional: theta_hat=n^{-1}sum_{i in U_n}[I_i(a)Y_i^{obs}/pi_i(a)-I_i(b)Y_i^{obs}/pi_i(b)], with |theta_hat-theta|<=sqrt{2 kappa_n Vbar_n log(2/alpha)/n}+kappa_n log(2/alpha)/(3n pi_exp)

bibliography.citation: Aronow, P. M. and C. Samii (2017). Estimating average causal effects under general interference, with application to a social network experiment. Annals of Applied Statistics 11, 1912-1947.
Rigdon, J. and M. G. Hudgens (2015). Exact confidence intervals in the presence of interference. Statistics & Probability Letters 105, 130-135.
Tchetgen Tchetgen, E. J. and T. J. VanderWeele (2012). On causal inference in the presence of interference. Statistical Methods in Medical Research 21, 55-75.
Pelekis, C., J. Ramon, and Y. Wang (2017). Hölder-type inequalities and their applications to concentration and correlation bounds. Indagationes Mathematicae 28, 170--182.
Janson, S. (2004). Large deviations for sums of partly dependent random variables. Random Structures & Algorithms 24, 234-248.
Sandoval, R. J., S. Balakrishnan, A. Feller, M. I. Jordan, and I. Waudby-Smith (2026). On nonasymptotic confidence intervals for treatment effects in randomized experiments. arXiv:2601.11744.
Leung, M. P. (2022). Causal inference under approximate neighborhood interference. Econometrica 90, 267-293.
Harshaw, C., J. A. Middleton, and F. Sävje (2026). Optimized variance estimation under interference and complex experimental designs. Journal of the American Statistical Association.
Ham, D. W., I. Bojinov, M. Lindon, and M. Tingley (2022). Design-based confidence sequences for causal inference. arXiv:2210.08639.
Kandiros, V., C. Pipis, C. Daskalakis, and C. Harshaw (2024). The conflict graph design: Estimating causal effects under arbitrary neighborhood interference. arXiv:2411.10908.

tldr: For design-based exposure effects under Bernoulli network experiments, the paper pairs a computable fractional-chromatic Bernstein interval, valid over the full nonhomogeneous exposure class, with an exact homogeneous-witness transport that attains the effective rare-exposure count and yields a same-class expected-length sandwich; the concentration mechanism itself is established substrate, not the novelty claim.

project_justification.gap: The unresolved comparison is confidence-interval length for rare network exposures, not the existence of exposure-HT or dependency-graph concentration. Aronow--Samii provides the design-based exposure framework, exposure-HT estimation, and variance-based interference inference, but not this expected-length lower bound or same-class sandwich. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang provides the exact-fold Holder aggregation used on the upper side, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) provides generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein-type large deviations for bounded partly dependent sums in terms of \(\chi^*\), summed variances, and a summand bound; neither supplies an exposure-HT causal interval, an e-value, the exact rare-revelation reduction, or the confidence-length comparison. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith assumes SUTVA and ordinary direct two-arm outcomes under Bernoulli or complete randomization and proves information-theoretic minimax RMSE rates of order \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\), rather than uniformly valid interval expected-length lower bounds carrying \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\); it has neither network-exposure modeling nor the exact transport to independent rare reveals, although its effective-count intuition is consistent with \(B=n/r\), \(q=2^{-r}\), and rate \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\) after the present homogeneous-witness reduction. Leung (2022) studies approximate neighborhood interference and asymptotic inference; Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026) optimizes variance estimation under interference and complex designs; Ham et al. (2022) develops design-based confidence sequences; Kandiros et al. (2024) studies conflict-graph design and estimation under arbitrary neighborhood interference; Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele gives partial-interference benchmarks, including finite-sample concentration-based inference; and Rigdon--Hudgens gives exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference. None of those results establishes the present fixed-confidence, balanced-Bernoulli clique-block, rare-exposure expected-length lower bound and attained same-class comparison. The scope distinction is essential: upper validity is over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, whereas exact compression and the lower bound use only the homogeneous witness subclass.
project_justification.niche: The paper occupies the narrow junction between an established upper concentration mechanism and a new decision-theoretic witness argument. Aronow--Samii supplies the causal exposure and exposure-HT setting; exact-fold Holder aggregation from Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and generic \(\chi^*\)-Bernstein concentration from Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) explain the upper tail substrate, for which no novelty is claimed. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies compatible \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) effective-count intuition under no interference, but its stated RMSE theorems do not imply a uniformly valid interval-length lower bound with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), network exposures, or the exact rare-reveal transport; in the witness, \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) recover the aligned scale \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\). The niche also differs from Leung's approximate-interference asymptotics, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's variance-estimation optimization, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design problem, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference concentration benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary-outcome intervals. Thus the niche is not a new fractional-color Bernstein theorem, a general-network minimax result, or separate necessity of graph and positivity penalties. Throughout this positioning, upper validity remains nonhomogeneous, while exact compression and lower-bound witnessing are homogeneous-subclass arguments.
project_justification.fill: The paper fills that niche by transporting a block-homogeneous witness exactly to independent rare revelations and using it to certify the lower endpoint of an attained expected-length sandwich, while the fractional-color interval supplies the upper endpoint over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class. The lower witness is a subclass argument: upper validity is genuinely nonhomogeneous, whereas exact compression and the lower bound use only homogeneous target schedules. Relative to Aronow--Samii, the added object is the lower bound and same-class comparison, not the exposure-HT framework. Relative to Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3), the addition is the causal specialization together with exact witness transport and decision-theoretic interval-length analysis, not Holder or fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration. Relative to Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith, the result is aligned with effective count \(Bq\) but is not a corollary because their SUTVA RMSE theorems do not furnish network-exposure interval-length risk or its \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) dependence. Relative to Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens, it adds the fixed balanced-Bernoulli rare-exposure lower-bound transport and same-class sandwich, rather than asymptotic approximate-interference inference, optimal variance estimation, confidence sequences, conflict-graph design, partial-interference concentration alone, or exact two-stage binary intervals.

related_work: Aronow--Samii is the foundation for design-based exposure mappings, exposure-HT estimators, and variance-based inference under interference; it does not prove the present confidence-length lower bound or attained same-class sandwich. The exact-fold Holder aggregation used for the upper bound comes from Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, and no novelty is claimed for that mechanism. Janson (2004), especially Theorem 2.3, gives generic Bernstein-type large-deviation bounds for bounded partly dependent sums using the fractional chromatic number \(\chi^*\), summed variances, and a uniform summand bound. Janson's theorem is therefore close upper-concentration substrate, but it is not an exposure-HT causal interval or e-value and contains neither the exact rare-revelation reduction nor a confidence-length lower or minimax comparison. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith assumes SUTVA, with ordinary direct two-arm outcomes under Bernoulli or complete randomization, and proves information-theoretic minimax RMSE estimation rates of order \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\). It does not prove uniformly valid confidence-interval expected-length lower bounds with the factor \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), and its stated model and theorems provide neither network-exposure modeling nor the exact transport to independent rare reveals. Nevertheless, after the homogeneous-witness reduction here, \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) make its \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\) effective-count intuition fully consistent with the present scale; the present result is aligned with that intuition, not a corollary of its stated theorems. Leung (2022) treats approximate neighborhood interference and asymptotic inference, rather than a finite-sample rare-exposure expected-length sandwich. Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026) optimizes variance estimation under interference and complex designs, not confidence-length minimax risk or an exact rare-revelation reduction. Ham et al. (2022) develops design-based confidence sequences, not the fixed-confidence interference-class expected-length lower bound and same-class comparison here. Kandiros et al. (2024) studies conflict-graph experimental design and estimation under arbitrary neighborhood interference, rather than uniformly valid confidence-length risk for the fixed balanced-Bernoulli clique-block experiment. Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele supplies partial-interference causal benchmarks, including finite-sample concentration-based inference, but not this rare-exposure expected-length lower bound. Rigdon--Hudgens supplies exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference, but not this lower bound or sandwich. Throughout, upper validity ranges over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, while exact compression and the lower bound use only the homogeneous witness subclass. The contribution is the exact witness transport plus the attained same-class confidence-length sandwich, not fractional-color Bernstein concentration, a general-network minimax theorem, separate necessity of graph and exposure penalties, or a claim that prior effective-count intuition is inapplicable.
interpretation: The fractional-chromatic radius behaves as though the experiment had an effective number of independent exposure opportunities, but the paper's novelty claim is deliberately narrower than that heuristic. Aronow--Samii already supplies the exposure-HT causal framework and variance-based interference inference. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang supplies exact-fold Holder aggregation, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) supplies generic \(\chi^*\)-adaptive Bernstein large deviations for bounded partly dependent sums; these are upper-bound substrates, not claimed discoveries. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith proves \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) minimax RMSE behavior under SUTVA and ordinary two-arm Bernoulli or complete randomization. Its theorems do not directly yield network-exposure confidence-length risk with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) or the exact independent-rare-reveal transport, even though substituting \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) after the homogeneous-witness reduction gives the same effective-count intuition \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\). Leung's approximate-interference asymptotics, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's optimal variance estimation, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design and estimation, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference concentration benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary intervals address different inferential or design objects. The correct reading is therefore: the interval is valid on the full nonhomogeneous clique-block class, exact compression and the lower bound are established on its homogeneous witness subclass, and that witness certifies an attained same-class expected-length sandwich. It is not a new Holder--Bernstein mechanism, not a general-network minimax theorem, not proof that graph and exposure penalties are separately necessary, and not a rejection of earlier effective-count intuition.
technical_internal_limitation: The observable envelope replaces the schedule-specific design variance and can be conservative, while the generic upper radius combines fractional coloring and exposure positivity without proving that those two penalties are separately necessary on every network. The decision-theoretic lower analysis is certified through a balanced clique-block construction: the interval is valid over the larger nonhomogeneous block class, but exact data compression and the lower bound use only a block-homogeneous witness subclass. Consequently the sandwich is a same-class result by subclass witnessing, not a general-network minimax characterization. This boundary is compatible with the comparator map: Aronow--Samii covers exposure-HT foundations; Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson cover the upper concentration substrate; Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies aligned effective-count intuition under SUTVA but not the stated interval-length theorem; and Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens study, respectively, approximate-interference asymptotics, variance-estimation optimization, confidence sequences, conflict-graph design, partial-interference concentration, and exact two-stage binary intervals rather than this rare-reveal expected-length sandwich.
honest_scope: The finite-sample upper interval applies to bounded outcomes under known Bernoulli assignment, correct neighborhood exposure mappings, target-exposure positivity, and the stated exposure-dependency graph. The confidence-length comparison specializes to the canonical balanced clique-block class. Its upper validity covers nonhomogeneous schedules, whereas its exact reduction and lower bound deliberately use only homogeneous target schedules as witnesses. Aronow--Samii, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) already cover, respectively, the causal exposure-HT framework, exact-fold Holder aggregation, and generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith gives aligned \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) effective-count RMSE intuition under SUTVA, but not this network-exposure interval-length result or its \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) factor. Leung (2022), Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026), Ham et al. (2022), Kandiros et al. (2024), Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens do not state this fixed balanced-Bernoulli rare-revelation lower bound and same-class comparison. Accordingly, the claimed contribution is only the exact witness transport and the attained same-class expected-length sandwich; it excludes novelty for fractional-color Bernstein concentration, a general-network minimax theorem, separate necessity of graph and positivity penalties, and any assertion that prior effective-count intuition cannot apply conceptually.
